title: Week 13 binary-heavy workflow risk register
acceptanceConclusion: Binary-heavy workflows must be planned around memory, disk, database, pruning, object storage, wait states, AI calls, browser automation, backup, and restore rather than only around CPU and RAM.

risks.risk: Default memory binary mode can crash the process when large files or many simultaneous files are processed.
symptoms: Memory RSS spikes during file steps
Container restarts or process exits
Large binary workflows fail before completion
mitigation: Set N8N_DEFAULT_BINARY_DATA_MODE=filesystem for single-instance production file workflows
Use database or S3 mode when queue mode or multi-worker architecture requires shared binary access
Limit file size at the trigger or upload layer
risks.verification: Run a file-heavy test workflow and confirm memory stays under the configured alert threshold.

risks.risk: Filesystem binary data moves pressure from memory to disk and can fill the persistent volume.
symptoms: binaryData directory grows every day
Disk usage warning or host volume full
Backups become slower as binary files accumulate
mitigation: Use persistent storage rather than ephemeral filesystem
Alert on disk usage and binary storage growth
Tune execution pruning and retention windows
risks.verification: Confirm pruning lowers active binary storage for the currently active binary data mode.

risks.risk: Database binary mode and saved execution data can expand PostgreSQL storage, backup windows, and restore time.
symptoms: PostgreSQL storage grows faster than execution count explains
Backup duration grows week over week
Database I/O rises during normal file workflows
mitigation: Disable unnecessary success execution data saving
Set EXECUTIONS_DATA_MAX_AGE and EXECUTIONS_DATA_PRUNE_MAX_COUNT intentionally
Move large binary data to S3 when enterprise external storage is available
risks.verification: Restore from backup into a test database and measure whether the restore time is acceptable for the stated RTO.

risks.risk: Changing binary data modes can leave old objects in a previous storage location and make pruning expectations wrong.
symptoms: S3 bucket grows even after execution pruning
Filesystem storage shrinks while object storage does not
Old executions reference binary objects in a mode that is no longer active
mitigation: Document the active binary data mode before and after migration
Apply bucket lifecycle policies that match retention requirements
Perform a mode migration checklist and object restore test
risks.verification: Pick an older execution with binary data and confirm it can still retrieve the file after mode changes.

risks.risk: AI document workflows combine large input files, extraction, prompt payloads, model latency, retries, and large responses.
symptoms: AI steps dominate execution duration
Rate-limit and retry errors increase
Worker memory grows during document extraction
mitigation: Chunk documents before model calls
Set timeouts and retry backoff
Run AI-heavy workflows on dedicated workers
risks.verification: Benchmark representative documents rather than tiny samples before declaring capacity sufficient.

risks.risk: Browser automation can start heavyweight host or worker processes that consume CPU, memory, screenshots, downloads, and temp storage.
symptoms: Worker memory spikes during browser steps
Long-running browser processes remain after execution
Download or screenshot directories grow unexpectedly
mitigation: Run browser automation on a dedicated worker pool
Limit concurrency for browser workflows
Set process timeout and cleanup temp files after each execution
risks.verification: Run concurrent browser jobs and confirm no process or temp file remains after completion.

risks.risk: Waiting executions preserve state and are not eligible for normal pruning while waiting.
symptoms: Waiting executions accumulate over days
Database and binary storage do not shrink after pruning
Old paused workflow payloads remain available
mitigation: Avoid carrying large binary data into long waits
Set explicit waiting workflow limits and review cadence
Move large files to external object storage and pass references through the wait
risks.verification: List waiting executions and confirm their payload size and age are within policy.

risks.risk: Large webhook payloads create immediate memory and response-time pressure before downstream processing can be offloaded.
symptoms: Webhook response p95 rises during file uploads
Provider retries increase
Queue backlog grows immediately after bursts
mitigation: Use signed upload URLs or object storage references for large files
Respond quickly and process heavy work asynchronously
Set concurrency limits for production executions
risks.verification: Load test realistic webhook payload sizes and confirm response time stays within provider timeout.

risks.risk: A safe file workflow can become unsafe when many executions run at the same time.
symptoms: Failures appear only during bursts
CPU and memory rise together
Database connections spike
mitigation: Set N8N_CONCURRENCY_PRODUCTION_LIMIT in regular mode
Use queue mode with Redis and workers for sustained concurrency
Separate heavy and light workload classes
risks.verification: Run burst tests at the expected peak and confirm latency, memory, and database connections remain within policy.

risks.risk: Large execution and binary data increase backup size and can make restore too slow for the business RTO.
symptoms: Backup window overlaps business hours
Restore drill takes longer than accepted downtime
Object storage and database backups are not coordinated
mitigation: Define RPO and RTO before increasing retention
Test restore with database and binary data together
Use retention and lifecycle policies to keep backup size predictable
risks.verification: Complete a restore drill from the latest backup and document the measured restore duration.
